summary:
The Boer Wars were two conflicts fought between the British Empire and the Boer (Afrikaner) republics of the Transvaal and the Orange Free State in southern Africa, the first in 1880–81 and the second from 1899 to 1902. The Second Boer War, far larger and more brutal than the first, was one of Britain's most costly and controversial imperial wars and generated significant criticism of British conduct both at home and internationally.

The Boers were the descendants of Dutch, German, and French Huguenot settlers who had moved into the interior of southern Africa in the [[event:3638|Great Trek]] of the 1830s and 1840s, establishing independent republics away from British colonial control at the Cape. The discovery of diamonds at Kimberley in 1867 and gold on the Witwatersrand in 1886 transformed the geopolitical stakes, making the Boer republics enormously wealthy and attracting a flood of British immigrants (uitlanders) who the Boer governments refused to enfranchise.

British imperialist ambitions, particularly those of Cecil Rhodes and Colonial Secretary Joseph Chamberlain, sought to bring the Boer republics into a South African federation under British control. The disastrous Jameson Raid of 1895–96 — an unauthorized British filibuster into the Transvaal intended to trigger an uitlander uprising — failed humiliatingly but heightened tensions. War began in October 1899 when the Boers issued an ultimatum and invaded British colonial territory before a British military buildup was complete.

The Boers' guerrilla tactics and knowledge of the terrain proved remarkably effective against numerically superior British forces. Britain eventually committed over 450,000 troops to the conflict. British commander Lord Kitchener responded to the guerrilla campaign by burning Boer farms, confiscating livestock, and — most controversially — interning Boer civilians and Black Africans in concentration camps where disease and inadequate supplies caused approximately 27,000 Boer and at least 20,000 Black African deaths.

The Peace of Vereeniging in May 1902 ended the war. The Boer republics were annexed by Britain, but Boer political autonomy was effectively restored within a few years as Britain sought reconciliation with the Afrikaner population. The price was the abandonment of the Black African population, whose political and civil rights were sacrificed to achieve white unity in the new Union of South Africa formed in 1910 — laying the foundations for apartheid.
